title: The Defense ETF Nobody's Talking About Is Quietly Crushing the Market
subtitle: SHLD has returned nearly 19% YTD — doubling the S&P 500 and leaving the Nasdaq in the dust while most retail investors were still chasing AI.
summary: The Global X Defense Tech ETF (SHLD) is on track for its best year since inception, outperforming the S&P 500 by nearly 10 points. With top holdings RTX, General Dynamics, and Northrop Grumman posting blowout earnings and a NATO spending supercycle underway, the quietest corner of the market is suddenly the loudest. Here's why SHLD deserves a hard look before the herd arrives.

links.title: Global X Defense Tech ETF — Fund Page

bodyHtml:
If you blinked, you missed it. While Wall Street spent the first five months of 2026 arguing about whether AI infrastructure spending was peaking or just getting started, a much quieter rotation was happening in plain sight. The Global X Defense Tech ETF — ticker SHLD — has returned +18.7% year-to-date through May 31. That's more than double the S&P 500 (+9.2%) and more than two and a half times the Nasdaq 100 (+7.8%). Not bad for an ETF most retail investors have never heard of.

What Is SHLD, and Why Is It Winning?

Launched in December 2021, SHLD is the only pure-play defense technology ETF on the market. It doesn't own airlines. It doesn't bundle commercial aerospace with military. It targets companies deriving the majority of their revenue from defense, aerospace, and government contracting. The top five holdings tell the story: RTX Corporation at 15.2%, General Dynamics at 12.8%, Northrop Grumman at 11.4%, Lockheed Martin at 10.9%, and L3Harris at 9.3%. Collectively, these five names account for nearly 60% of the fund and they are all firing on multiple cylinders in 2026.

Compare that to peer ETFs like ITA (iShares U.S. Aerospace & Defense) or PPA (Invesco Aerospace & Defense), which both carry heavier commercial aerospace weightings. While those funds have posted respectable +16.3% and +15.9% YTD respectively, they can't match SHLD's concentrated defense exposure. The SPDR S&P Aerospace & Defense ETF (XAR) lags further at +14.8%, diluted by a broader index approach.

The Earnings Backdrop Is Stunning

RTX Corporation kicked off first-quarter earnings season on April 28 with revenue of $21.3 billion, up 8% year-over-year, and adjusted EPS of $1.84 — a beat of $0.12. Pratt & Whitney engine deliveries jumped 12%. Management raised full-year guidance, citing record demand across both its Collins Aerospace and Raytheon segments.

General Dynamics followed on April 24 with $11.7 billion in revenue (+6%) and EPS of $3.62, beating by $0.15. The company's backlog now stands at $94 billion, anchored by Gulfstream business jet orders and continued demand for its combat systems division. Northrop Grumman delivered the strongest beat of the group on April 23: $10.5 billion in revenue, EPS of $6.45, beating estimates by $0.20. Missile systems and space segments were the standout performers, pushing the backlog past $80 billion.

Even Lockheed Martin, which posted a slight EPS miss of $0.04 on April 18 due to F-35 supply chain friction, still grew revenue 5% to $17.2 billion and maintained full-year guidance. Critically, Lockheed secured $6 billion in new international orders for its THAAD missile defense system during the quarter — a sign that demand for strategic defense systems has entered a new phase.

The Macro Tailwind Nobody's Discounting

This isn't just good company performance. The structural backdrop for defense spending has shifted in ways that feel closer to the Reagan buildup than anything we've seen since. In April 2026, NATO allies formally agreed to raise defense spending targets to 3.5% of GDP by 2030, and approved an immediate €100 billion European Defense Fund. That's a massive incremental demand signal for practically every name in SHLD's portfolio.

At home, the Pentagon's FY2027 budget request of $895 billion — up 7% year-over-year — prioritizes hypersonics, missile defense, space operations, and cyber warfare. Those are precisely the subsectors where SHLD's holdings have competitive moats. President Biden's administration and congressional leadership from both parties have shown rare bipartisan alignment on defense spending increases.

Geopolitical catalysts haven't slowed down either. The war in Ukraine grinds on, driving sustained ammunition and systems procurement. U.S.-China tensions over Taiwan escalated in May with large-scale naval exercises in the South China Sea, further underscoring the need for naval and aerial deterrence capabilities. Japan, South Korea, and Australia all announced defense budget increases of 8-12% for their next fiscal years.

Competition and Risks

No pitch is complete without the other side. SHLD carries an expense ratio of 0.50%, which is reasonable for a thematic ETF but higher than broad-market index funds. The fund's $3.2 billion in AUM is still modest — liquidity can be thinner during market dislocations. And there is real concentration risk: the top three holdings alone represent nearly 40% of the fund.

The biggest risk is political. Defense stocks have historically been vulnerable to drawdowns during ceasefire negotiations or surprise budget deals. If U.S.-Russia talks on Ukraine unexpectedly lead to a freeze, or if Congress delivers a flat defense budget, the sector could give back some gains. Valuation is also creeping up: SHLD's weighted portfolio P/E sits around 24x forward earnings, above its 3-year average of 21x.

But here's the thing about defense supercycles: they tend to last longer than the market expects. The Cold War buildup ran for nearly a decade. Post-9/11 defense spending expanded for 10 years straight. This cycle has the tailwinds of great-power competition, technology modernization, and alliance burden-sharing — all of which argue for multi-year, not multi-quarter, duration.

The Signal Take

SHLD is quietly proving that sector rotation doesn't have to mean jumping into small-cap value or exiting tech entirely. It means finding the sectors where structural spending commitments create a floor under earnings and the ceiling is higher than consensus assumes. Defense is that sector in 2026.

For retail investors, SHLD offers something rare: concentrated exposure to a theme that is both growing and under-owned by the ETF crowd. The fund's 4:1 stock split, effective June 2, signals that Global X expects continued retail interest. Watch the Pentagon budget markup process this summer, keep an eye on NATO's capability pledges, and pay attention to Q2 earnings calls at RTX and NOC — those will be the catalysts that tell us whether this outperformance has legs for the second half.

The defense ETF nobody's talking about? It might be the best trade you make all year.

This article was written on June 1, 2026. The Signal is not a registered investment adviser. Do your own research.
